headlines.geopolitics: Russian propaganda documentary wins Oscar, sparks ethical debate
headlines.economy: Nvidia unveils new AI products at massive developer conference
headlines.domestic: Afghan national arrested after conviction for exposing himself to minor

metadata.daily_do: Check if your local school district has clear policies on documenting concerning behavior - not for surveillance, but for protection

clips.caption: When Duke realizes the vetting process might actually be broken
Professor Pringle drops WWII knowledge about propaganda films
Scientists used WHAT for cicada research?

script.text: Look, I served two tours and I'm telling you right now — if we can't properly vet people who literally fought alongside us, then our entire immigration system is completely broken.
It's Monday, March 16th. I'm Leo Barros and this is The Friction. Let's get you caught up. First, geopolitics.
A documentary called 'Mr. Nobody Against Putin' won an Oscar last night. The film documents how Putin's regime is systematically indoctrinating Russian schoolchildren with wartime propaganda.
Oh wow.
The filmmakers say they faced what they called 'an ethical minefield' — how do you document authoritarian propaganda without amplifying it?
Fascinating question.

Economy next. Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang unveiled new AI products at their annual developer conference, leveraging recent acquisitions to show how artificial intelligence is, quote, 'changing everything.'
Everything except my bank account.
Ha!
And domestic. DHS arrested an Afghan national who was admitted under Biden's Operation Allies Welcome program. He was convicted of indecent exposure to a minor.
Jesus.
The case highlights ongoing concerns about vetting processes for the nearly 190,000 Afghan nationals admitted under that program, which Trump has now suspended.
Suspended? About time.
Duke, come on.
We're going to dig into that Afghan resettlement story because it touches on some really fundamental questions about how we balance humanitarian obligations with security concerns.
Alright, let's talk about this. Basir Ahmad Safi, an Afghan national in his thirties, was arrested by DHS on March 11th following his conviction for indecent exposure to a minor.
He was originally charged with lewd exhibition, soliciting a child via computer, unlawful use of communications devices, and child abuse. He was admitted in 2021 under Operation Allies Welcome.
Okay, first of all, that's horrifying. But we need to be really careful about how we frame this story.
Careful how? The guy was admitted under a program with what DHS now admits was inadequate vetting, and he hurt a kid.
I'm not defending him, Duke. I'm saying we can't use one case — one terrible case — to justify shutting the door on 190,000 people who risked their lives helping American forces.
One case? Leo, didn't you mention another incident?
Yeah, there was Rahmanullah Lakanwal, also admitted under Allies Welcome, who shot two National Guard soldiers in D.C. One of them, Specialist Sarah Beckstrom, died. She was twenty years old.
That's... that's awful. But Duke, you know statistics better than this.
What statistics?
Two cases out of 190,000 people. That's a 0.001% rate. American citizens commit crimes at much higher rates than that.
Bree, those are the cases we know about. And more importantly — American citizens, we're stuck with. These were people we chose to let in.
We chose to let them in because they helped us! Interpreters, drivers, people who worked on bases. They had targets on their backs because of us.
I know that. I worked with some of these guys. But that doesn't mean we throw security protocols out the window.
Can I ask a dumb question? What exactly does 'unvetted referrals' mean?
That's not dumb. It means people were referred for admission without going through the normal background check process.
So like, someone just vouched for them?
Pretty much. And look, I get it — when Kabul fell, it was chaos. But that's exactly when you need systems in place.
But what was the alternative, Duke? Leave them to die? Because that's what happened to a lot of people who helped us.
The alternative was having a plan. We knew for twenty years we were going to leave eventually. We should have had robust vetting systems ready to go.
Okay, that's fair. But suspending the entire program now? That's punishing 189,998 people for the actions of two.
It's not punishment, it's— look, if you find out your security system has holes, you pause and fix it. You don't keep running it and hope for the best.
Duke raises an interesting point about systems. Bree, is there data on how other refugee programs have handled this balance?
Yeah, actually. The post-Vietnam boat people program had similar challenges. They processed over a million refugees, and yes, there were some bad actors.
But they didn't shut it down. They improved the vetting. And those refugees became some of our most successful immigrant communities.
But Bree, Vietnam refugees weren't coming from a country where we'd been fighting an insurgency for twenty years. The threat profile is completely different.
That's... actually a good point.
Thank you.
So where does that leave us? We have people who helped American forces who are still in danger, but we also have legitimate security concerns.
I think Duke's right about pausing to fix the system. I just don't want the pause to become permanent.
Look, I served two tours and I'm telling you right now — if we can't properly vet people who literally fought alongside us, then our entire immigration system is completely broken.
These aren't random asylum seekers. We have records. We know who they are. If we can't get that right, what hope do we have for anything else?
That's actually terrifying when you put it that way.
Alright, let's get some historical context on this. Professor?
Well, Leo, this reminds me quite directly of the displaced persons crisis after World War II. Between 1945 and 1952, the United States admitted over 400,000 European refugees.
The program faced enormous controversy because among those refugees were former Nazi collaborators who had lied about their backgrounds.
Oh wow.
In 1948, it came to light that hundreds — possibly thousands — of war criminals had entered the country by falsifying their identities and histories.
Wait, war criminals just... walked in?
Indeed. The vetting process was overwhelmed by the sheer volume and the chaos of post-war Europe. Sound familiar?
What did they do about it?
They created the Office of Special Investigations in 1979 — thirty years later — specifically to hunt down Nazi war criminals who had entered illegally.
But here's what's crucial: they never stopped the refugee program. They reformed it. Improved the screening. Made it more rigorous.
See?
However, Miss Aldana, there's an important difference. The Nazi collaborators were hiding in plain sight among genuine refugees. In Afghanistan, the risk profile was known from the beginning.
Exactly.
The lesson, I believe, is that humanitarian crises don't excuse abandoning security protocols. They require adapting them.
That's a really helpful framework, Professor. The question becomes how long is reasonable for that adaptation process.
Indeed. Though I should note — the most successful refugee integration in American history, the Cuban exiles after 1959, had the benefit of existing intelligence networks and cultural familiarity.
Afghanistan presents neither of those advantages.
But Doc, we did have twenty years of working relationships with these people. That should count for something.
Quite right. Though relationships can be exploited as well as trusted. That's the eternal dilemma of intelligence work.
Arré, that's what I've been trying to say.
Alright, before we all get too depressed, let's hit some of the weirder stuff that happened this weekend.
First up: Florida man punches teenager after failed car burglary. His defense? He was 'looking for his lady.'
Was she in the car?
She was not. She was, apparently, a concept.
I can't.
Next: scientists have proven that people who say 'circle back' and 'synergize' are actually bad at their jobs.
Finally, peer review.
The study found that people who use corporate buzzwords perform worse on actual task completion. Shocking absolutely no one who's ever been in a meeting.
In my day, we called that 'all hat and no cattle.'
Perfect. Speaking of all hat and no cattle — a Washington man got a $1.8 million bill from DHS for overstaying his visa.
A million eight? What did he do, rent the Statue of Liberty?
Apparently it's a daily fine that compounds. He overstayed by like three years.
That seems... proportional.
And finally, in news that will haunt my dreams: scientists conducting cicada research in the rainforest used improvised condoms in their study and discovered weird mating behavior.
I'm sorry, what?
They were studying cicada reproduction and ran out of proper equipment, so they used, um, prophylactics to collect samples. And it worked so well they discovered a new behavior pattern.
Science finds a way.
I have so many questions and I want answers to exactly none of them.
The paper is published in Nature if you're curious. I am not including that in our show notes.
Scientific innovation has always required creative problem-solving. Though this is perhaps more creative than most.
Alright, your Daily Do. Today's Afghan resettlement story got me thinking about documentation and protection.
Here's something you can actually do: check if your local school district has clear policies on documenting concerning behavior.
Not for surveillance, but for protection. When adults notice warning signs — whether from a student, parent, or volunteer — there should be clear protocols for reporting and follow-up.
Most districts have these policies buried on their websites. Find them. Read them. Make sure they exist and make sense.
Because whether we're talking about immigration vetting or school safety, the principle is the same: good systems protect everyone.
A final thought from Edmund Burke: 'The only thing necessary for the triumph of evil is for good men to do nothing.' Documentation is action.
That's The Friction. We're back tomorrow with more news, more arguments, and hopefully fewer cicada facts. I'm Jax.
Leo Barros. Thanks for hanging with us.
